required.title: [For Mentor] The Whole-Brain Child
required.author: Daniel J. Siegel and Tina Payne Bryson

required.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Stories of courage appropriate to age

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] The Courage of Sarah Noble
suggested.author: Alice Dalgliesh

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] The Story of Ruby Bridges
suggested.author: Robert Coles

supplementary.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Amos Fortune, Free Man
supplementary.author: Elizabeth Yates

competence_markers.description: They speak up in situations where they could remain silent — asking questions, naming wrongs, admitting mistakes — with increasing frequency
They return to activities or situations after failure rather than permanently avoiding them
They do the hard thing first on most days, without being reminded
They approach mild fears rather than avoiding them, showing willingness to take steps toward what frightens them even if they do not complete the approach every time
They can articulate that courage is not the absence of fear but acting in its presence
They recognize courage in stories and in the people around them — they can name what made an action courageous
They engage honestly in the weekly courage review, naming both moments of courage and moments of avoidance
The list of things they avoid due to fear is getting shorter over time, not longer

narration_prompts.detail: Explain it in your own words. Is courage the same as not being afraid? Why or why not?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you were afraid and did the thing anyway.
narration_prompts.detail: What were you afraid of? What did you do? How did you feel before? How did you feel after?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you avoided something because you were afraid.
narration_prompts.detail: What were you avoiding? What happened because you avoided it? If you could go back, what would you do differently?

narration_prompts.prompt: Who is the most courageous person you know or have heard about?
narration_prompts.detail: What makes them courageous? Were they ever afraid?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the hard thing you do first?
narration_prompts.detail: Why is it hard? Does doing it first make the rest of the day easier?

narration_prompts.prompt: What are you still afraid of?
narration_prompts.detail: Is that fear getting smaller or bigger? What could you do about it?

cross_connections.description: Courage is traditionally the first of the four cardinal virtues — the one that makes all the others possible. A person can know what is just, temperate, and wise, but without courage, they will not act on any of it when the cost is real. IC3.5 builds the experiential foundation for what IC1.2 teaches conceptually.

Speaking up — especially about your own mistakes — is the earliest form of integrity. The child who learns to say "I did that" when it is hard to admit is building the same capacity that IC1.3 will develop formally.
Fear is an emotion. The ability to name it, feel it, and still act is emotional regulation in its most consequential form. IC3.5 and IC3.1 reinforce each other directly.
The fear approach practices in IC3.5 are a specific form of the controlled stress exposure IC3.4 develops. The child who approaches a mild fear is stress-inoculating themselves.
Leadership requires the willingness to act when others will not. That willingness is courage. The child who builds it now will have it available when leadership demands it later.
The ability to hold an independent position when the group disagrees requires social courage. IC3.5 builds this capacity from the earliest tier, beginning with the simple act of speaking up.

content.why_this_matters:
Everyone is afraid of something. That is not a problem. That is normal. Being afraid is what your body does when something is hard or unknown or uncomfortable. Fear is a signal — it tells you to pay attention.

But here is the thing most people never learn: **being afraid and doing the thing anyway is called courage.** Courage is not the same as not being afraid. A person who is not afraid is not being brave — they either do not understand the situation or they are being reckless. Courage is what happens when you feel the fear and you choose to act anyway because the thing is right or necessary or important.

You already know what this feels like. You have felt it when you were standing at the edge of something — a high dive, a dark room, a new group of kids — and part of you wanted to turn around and part of you stepped forward. That step forward is the whole lesson.

This packet is about learning to take that step more often. Not because the fear goes away. It does not go away. But because you get better at stepping forward even when it is there. And every time you do, you become a little bit stronger. Every time you avoid the hard thing, you become a little bit weaker. That is the simple truth at the center of courage: **it is trained, not inherited.** You build it the same way you build anything — by practicing.
content.what_this_develops:
This packet does not produce fearless children. It produces children who act in the presence of fear. Over time, these patterns become visible:

1. **Speaking up** — saying what is true or needed even when there is hesitation or shyness
2. **Trying again after failure** — returning to a situation that produced failure or embarrassment, rather than avoiding it permanently
3. **Doing the hard thing first** — choosing the difficult, unwanted task before the easy, preferred one
4. **Facing mild fears with support** — approaching the spider, the dark room, the unfamiliar situation rather than avoiding it — with an adult present but not doing the approaching for the child
5. **Recognizing courage in others** — seeing when someone in a story or in life did something brave, and understanding what made it brave
6. **Understanding that courage is not fearlessness** — the foundational distinction that separates real courage from recklessness
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Courage Really Is

Courage is not the absence of fear. This is the most important sentence in this packet, and it needs to be understood before anything else makes sense.

A person who is not afraid is not being courageous. They are either:
- **Ignorant** — they do not understand the danger or difficulty
- **Reckless** — they understand it and do not care

Neither of those is courage. Courage is a third thing entirely: **you understand the danger or difficulty, you feel the fear, and you choose to act anyway because the action is right or necessary.**

Think about it this way. Two children stand at the edge of a pond in winter. One does not know the ice is thin — he runs out and falls through. That is not courage. That is ignorance. The other knows the ice might break, feels afraid, and still goes out carefully because a younger child is stuck in the middle and needs help. That is courage. The fear is present. The action happens anyway. The reason for the action is good.

**Courage requires three things:**
1. Something real to be afraid of (even if it is small)
2. The feeling of fear or difficulty
3. The choice to act anyway because the action matters

Without the fear, there is no courage. A test that is easy for you does not require courage to take. A conversation with your best friend about what to eat for lunch does not require courage. But speaking up when you see something wrong — even something small — that requires courage, because the fear of what others will think is real.

### Part 2: Speaking Up

The first and most common form of courage for a young person is using your voice when you do not want to.

This does not mean being loud. It does not mean being rude. It means saying what is true, what is needed, or what is right, even when:
- You are shy
- You are not sure anyone will listen
- You think people might laugh
- You think people might be upset
- It is easier to say nothing

**Examples:**
- A friend is being mean to another child. You say, "Stop. That is not okay." You feel uncomfortable. You say it anyway.
- A grown-up asks you a question and you know the answer, but you are nervous about speaking in front of others. You answer anyway.
- You made a mistake — you broke something, you did not do what you were supposed to do. Instead of hiding it or hoping no one notices, you say what happened.
- You do not understand something and everyone else seems to understand. You raise your hand and ask.

Every one of these feels hard. Every one of these is a moment of courage. And every time you do one of them, the next time becomes a little easier. Not because the fear goes away. Because you have proven to yourself that you can act even when fear is there.

**The opposite of speaking up is silence.** Silence when you should speak becomes a habit very quickly. The child who learns to stay quiet when something is wrong becomes the adult who stays quiet when something is wrong. And that adult watches things happen that they should have stopped, and they know it, and it eats at them. Speaking up early — even about small things — builds the muscle you will need for the big things later.

### Part 3: Trying Again After Failure

You tried something and it did not work. Maybe you fell off the bike. Maybe you got the answer wrong in front of everyone. Maybe you tried to build something and it collapsed. Maybe you tried to make a friend and they were not interested.

The moment after failure is the moment that matters most. Not the failure itself — what you do next.

**Two paths:**
1. **Avoidance:** "I am never doing that again." The thing that was hard becomes the thing you do not do. Over time, your life gets smaller and smaller because every failure removes another thing from the list of things you are willing to try.
2. **Return:** "That did not work. I am going to try again." The thing that was hard remains on the list. You try again. Maybe you fail again. You try again. Eventually you succeed — or you do not, but you become the kind of person who does not let failure stop them.

Path 2 is courage. It is the courage to return to the scene of your failure and try again.

**This is trained.** The adult in your life will help by:
- Not making a big deal of the failure — it happened, it is information, now what?
- Expecting you to try again — not forcing, but expecting, the way they expect you to complete your chores
- Celebrating the return — not the success necessarily, but the fact that you came back and tried again

The goal is not that you never fail. The goal is that failure never stops you.

### Part 4: Doing the Hard Thing First

Every day has hard things and easy things. Courage is not only found in dramatic moments. It lives in the small daily choice to do the difficult thing before the comfortable thing.

- The homework before the game
- The chore before the play
- The practice before the free time
- The conversation you have been avoiding before the conversation you enjoy

**Why first?** Because if you do the easy thing first, the hard thing grows. It sits in the back of your mind getting bigger and heavier. By afternoon, the hard thing that would have taken ten minutes in the morning now feels impossible. You have been dreading it all day. The dread is worse than the thing.

Doing the hard thing first is a daily practice of courage. It is small courage. It is unglamorous. No one writes stories about the child who did their math before playing outside. But that small daily choice — the choice to walk toward difficulty instead of away from it — is the same choice, in miniature, that every courageous person in history has made. They walked toward the hard thing. They did not wait for it to come to them.

**Practice this every day.** When you wake up and think about your day, ask: "What is the hardest thing I have to do today?" Do that thing first. Not second. First. Then everything else feels easier, because the hardest thing is already done.

### Part 5: Facing Mild Fears

You are afraid of something. Maybe it is the dark. Maybe it is a spider. Maybe it is a dog. Maybe it is the deep end of the pool. Maybe it is sleeping away from home. Maybe it is talking to grown-ups you do not know.

These fears are real. They are not silly. Your body is telling you that something might be dangerous, and that is your body doing its job. The question is not whether the fear is there. The question is what you do with it.

**The approach, not the avoidance.**

When a child avoids the thing they are afraid of, the fear grows. Every time you walk around the dark hallway instead of through it, the dark hallway becomes a little scarier. Every time you refuse to pet the dog, dogs become a little more frightening. Avoidance feeds fear. This is not just a saying — it is how your brain works. The parts of your brain that produce fear get stronger every time the fear wins.

When a child approaches the thing they are afraid of — slowly, with support, one step at a time — the fear shrinks. Not all at once. Not completely. But each time you approach, your brain learns something new: "I did that, and I was okay." After enough of those experiences, the fear does not control you anymore. It might still be there, but it is small, and you are bigger.

**How this works in practice:**
- The adult is present. They do not do it for you, but they are there.
- You take one step toward the thing. Not all the steps at once. One step.
- You notice how you feel. Scared? That is normal. You are still standing.
- You take another step when you are ready.
- If you reach the thing and touch it, stand in it, do it — that is a victory. If you get partway and stop, that is also progress. The direction matters more than the distance.

**What the adult does NOT do:**
- Force you into the fear without preparation
- Tell you the fear is silly or not real
- Do the approach for you ("Here, I will hold the spider for you" — no, the child holds the spider)
- Rescue you at the first sign of discomfort

**What the adult DOES do:**
- Stay calm — their calm nervous system teaches yours
- Acknowledge the fear — "I can see this is hard for you"
- Expect the approach — not demand it harshly, but make clear that avoidance is not the plan
- Celebrate the attempt — "You walked halfway down that hallway. That took courage."

### Part 6: Celebrating the Attempt, Not Just the Success

This is crucial and easy to get wrong. Courage is about the choice to act, not about the outcome of the action.

The child who stands up to speak in front of the group and stumbles over their words has shown as much courage as the child who speaks perfectly. The child who tries to climb the wall and falls has shown as much courage as the child who reaches the top. The child who approaches the dog and then steps back at the last moment has shown more courage than the child who was never afraid of dogs in the first place.

**What courage looks like in this packet is the attempt.** The willingness to move toward the hard thing. The results will come with time and practice. But if we only celebrate results, the child learns that courage only counts when it succeeds — and that is a lesson that will make them avoid any situation where success is uncertain. Which is to say, every situation that actually requires courage.

The phrase to use: **"That took courage."** Not "good job" (which is about outcome). Not "you are so brave" (which is about identity as a fixed trait). "That took courage" names the thing that actually happened: the child made a choice that was hard, and they made it.
content.formative_practices:
These are not one-time activities. They are ongoing practices that build the courage this packet develops.

### Practice 1: The Hard Thing First [Daily]
Every morning, identify the hardest thing on your list for the day. Do it first. Before the things you want to do. Before the things that are easy. The hardest thing first. Track this weekly with your mentor: how many days did you do the hard thing first?

### Practice 2: Speak Up Moments [As They Arise]
When a situation arises where you could speak up or stay silent — a question you want to ask, a wrong you see, an answer you know but are nervous to give — speak up. Your mentor will help you notice these moments and will expect you to use your voice. After each one, briefly note: what did you say? Was it hard? What happened?

### Practice 3: The Return After Failure [As Opportunities Arise]
When you fail at something or have a bad experience with something, return to it. Not immediately if you need time. But return. Try the thing again. The bike, the math problem, the conversation, the physical challenge. The goal is not success on the return — it is the return itself.

### Practice 4: Fear Approach [Weekly or As Opportunities Arise]
With your mentor present, approach something that makes you afraid. It can be small — a dark room, an unfamiliar place, a physical challenge that intimidates you. Take one step closer than you did last time. Over weeks and months, the list of things you avoid should get shorter.

### Practice 5: Courage Stories [Weekly]
At least once a week, read or hear a story of someone who was afraid and acted anyway. After the story, discuss: What were they afraid of? What did they do? What would have happened if they had not acted? Were they afraid the whole time, or did the fear change as they acted? Would you have done what they did?

### Practice 6: Weekly Courage Review [Weekly, With Mentor]
At the end of each week, sit with your mentor for 5-10 minutes. Review: Where were you courageous this week? Where did you avoid something you should have faced? What was the hardest moment? What are you going to face next week? This is not a test. It is reflection — the beginning of knowing yourself.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Your Courage Is the Curriculum

Children learn courage the way they learn everything else at this age: by watching what the adults around them do. If you avoid difficult conversations, they learn that difficult conversations are things to be avoided. If you refuse to admit mistakes, they learn that admitting mistakes is dangerous. If you complain about hard tasks and put them off, they learn that hard tasks are to be dreaded and delayed.

Conversely, if you walk toward the hard thing — visibly, honestly, without drama — they learn that walking toward the hard thing is what people do. You do not need to be fearless. You need to be honest about your fear and act anyway, where the child can see it.

**Model the narration:** "I do not want to make this phone call. It is going to be uncomfortable. I am going to do it now and get it done." That sentence — spoken aloud in the child's presence — is worth more than any lecture on courage. The child hears: the adult is uncomfortable, the adult is doing it anyway, and the adult is not falling apart.

### The Distinction You Must Hold

There is a critical line between two things that look similar from the outside but are entirely different:

1. **Expecting courage:** "I know this is hard. I believe you can do it. I am here. Go ahead."
2. **Forcing compliance through shame:** "What are you, a baby? Just do it. Stop being scared."

The first produces courage. The second produces either compliance driven by shame (which is not courage — it is fear of a different kind) or withdrawal (the child learns that expressing fear is punished, so they hide it).

**The formula:**
- Acknowledge the fear: "I can see you are scared."
- Normalize the fear: "That is a normal thing to feel."
- State the expectation: "You are going to do it anyway."
- Provide presence: "I am right here."
- Celebrate the action: "That took courage."

Notice what is not in the formula: eliminating the fear. You are not trying to make the child unafraid. You are teaching them that fear is present and action is still possible.

### Do Not Rescue

The most common mistake is rescue — intervening at the first sign of the child's discomfort to remove the source of that discomfort. The child hesitates at the top of the slide, and the parent says, "You do not have to do it." The child struggles with a hard problem and the parent solves it for them. The child is nervous about speaking to a new person and the parent speaks for them.

Every rescue teaches the same lesson: **you cannot handle this, so I will handle it for you.** Over hundreds of repetitions, this produces a person who believes they cannot handle things. Who waits for rescue. Who avoids anything that might produce the need for rescue.

The alternative is not cruelty. It is patient expectation. You stay present. You stay calm. You do not force. But you do not rescue. You wait. The child finds their own way through — or they do not, today, and they try again tomorrow. Either way, they learn that you believe they are capable, and that belief becomes their own belief over time.

### When Fear Is Disproportionate

Some children have fears that are significantly out of proportion to the situation — terror of common animals, debilitating fear of the dark, extreme distress at separation. These may reflect normal developmental variation, or they may indicate something that needs clinical attention.

**The guideline:** If a fear is persistent, intensifying rather than improving, and significantly limiting the child's daily function, consult a professional. IC3.5 is designed for the normal range of childhood fears and the normal process of courage development. It is not a therapeutic intervention for anxiety disorders.

For the normal range: calm, consistent exposure with support and expectation. The child approaches, step by step, with you present. Progress is measured in months, not days.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Speaking Up

**Developing well:** The child uses their voice in situations where they could stay silent — asking questions, admitting mistakes, saying what they think. They may still be nervous, but they speak. The frequency is increasing over time.

**Developing slowly:** The child defaults to silence in any situation where speaking involves risk — social risk, risk of being wrong, risk of attention. They look to you to speak for them. They do not volunteer answers or admit mistakes unless cornered.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling speaking up yourself? Are you speaking for the child in situations where they should speak for themselves? Is the environment punishing for speech errors (laughter, correction, dismissal)? A child who is mocked for wrong answers will stop answering. Fix the environment.

### 2. Trying Again After Failure

**Developing well:** When the child fails at something — falls off the bike, gets the problem wrong, has a social experience that went badly — they return to it. Not necessarily immediately, but they return. The pattern is re-engagement, not permanent avoidance.

**Developing slowly:** Failure produces avoidance. The child will not try the thing again. The list of things they have failed at and will no longer attempt is growing rather than shrinking.

**Diagnostic questions:** How are you responding to failure? If failure produces parental disappointment, the child has two things to fear: the failure itself and your reaction. Ensure your response to failure is calm and forward-looking: "That did not work. What will you do differently next time?" Not: "You failed again."

### 3. Doing the Hard Thing First

**Developing well:** The child, with or without prompting, chooses to begin with the most difficult task of the day. They may not enjoy it, but they do it. The habit is forming.

**Developing slowly:** The child consistently avoids or delays the hardest task, filling time with easier or more pleasant activities. The hard thing is always last, or it is not done at all.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the hard thing clearly identified? The child may not know which thing is hardest. Help them identify it in the morning: "What is the hardest thing you have to do today? Do that first." Is the hard thing age-appropriate in difficulty? A task that is genuinely overwhelming is not a courage challenge — it is a task design problem. Simplify if needed.

### 4. Approaching Fears

**Developing well:** The child, with adult support, approaches things that frighten them. They may approach slowly. They may not complete the approach every time. But the direction is toward the fear, not away from it. Over months, specific fears are diminishing.

**Developing slowly:** The child refuses all approach. Avoidance is the only response to fear. New fears are developing. The child's world is getting smaller rather than larger.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you forcing rather than supporting? Forcing produces resistance, not courage. Are you rescuing? Rescuing produces dependence. The balance is: present, calm, expectant, patient. Are fears age-appropriate for approach work? A four-year-old can approach a dark room with you nearby. They are not being asked to sleep alone in the wilderness.

### 5. Understanding Courage

**Developing well:** The child can explain, in their own words, that courage means being afraid and doing the thing anyway. They can identify courage in stories and in their own life. They understand that not being afraid is not the same as being brave.

**Developing slowly:** The child equates courage with fearlessness — "brave people are not afraid." They cannot identify their own courageous moments because they were afraid during those moments and therefore do not classify them as brave.

**How to build this:** Consistent language. When the child does something hard, say "that took courage" — not "you were not even scared." When reading stories, ask: "Was this person afraid? Then how was it brave?" Repeat until the distinction is internalized.

### 6. Courage in Stories

**Developing well:** The child engages with courage stories actively. They can retell the story, identify what was hard, explain why the person acted, and connect it to their own experience. They have favorite courage stories that they return to.

**Developing slowly:** The child listens passively. They cannot retell. They do not connect the stories to their own life.

**How to improve engagement:** Ask better questions. Not "Did you like the story?" but "What was the scariest moment for that person? What would you have done?" Make the discussion personal. Share your own fears and your own courageous moments. The conversation is the practice.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Confusing Courage with Compliance
A child who does what they are told because they are afraid of punishment is not being courageous — they are being compliant from fear. Courage is choosing to act when the easier, safer path is available. If the child has no choice, there is no courage. Ensure the child can see the easier path and chooses the harder one anyway.

### Praising Fearlessness Instead of Courage
"You were not even scared!" is the wrong praise. It teaches the child that the goal is to not feel fear — which means they will begin hiding their fear rather than managing it. The right praise is: "You were scared, and you did it anyway. That is courage." This validates the fear and celebrates the action.

### Shame as a Motivator
"Are you really afraid of that?" Shame does not produce courage. It produces performance of fearlessness, which is fundamentally different. The child who is shamed into approaching a fear has not learned to manage fear — they have learned to fear your judgment more than they fear the thing. That is not progress. That is a different kind of fragility.

### Over-Protecting
The most common and most damaging pitfall. Every time you remove a challenge, prevent a failure, or shield the child from an age-appropriate fear, you are removing an opportunity for courage to develop. The child needs small, frequent opportunities to be afraid and act anyway. If their life contains no situations that produce fear or difficulty, courage cannot be trained.

### Making It Too Big
Courage for a four-year-old is walking into a dark room. It is not confronting a bully twice their size. Match the courage challenge to the child's age and development. Overwhelm does not build courage — it produces trauma or learned helplessness. The goal is a steady stream of small victories, not one dramatic test.

### Inconsistency
If you sometimes expect courage and sometimes allow avoidance based on your own energy level or mood, the child learns that the standard is negotiable. They will test it every time, because sometimes the test works. Maintain the standard: we approach, we do not avoid. Every time.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: What Courage Means
**Strong:** The child explains that courage means being afraid and doing the thing anyway. They may use their own words — "it is when you are scared but you still do it." They distinguish courage from fearlessness.
**Weak:** "Courage means being brave." No further explanation. No distinction from fearlessness.

### Prompt 2: A Time You Were Afraid and Did It Anyway
**Strong:** Specific event. Names the fear. Describes the action. Can articulate how they felt before and after. "I was afraid of the dog at Grandma's house but I walked up to it and petted it. My heart was beating really fast but then it licked my hand and I felt proud."
**Weak:** "I was brave one time." No specifics. Cannot recall a concrete event, or the event described does not actually involve fear.

### Prompt 3: A Time You Avoided Something
**Strong:** Honest. Names a specific situation they avoided. Can describe what happened because of the avoidance. May express regret or identify what they would do differently. This prompt requires the most self-awareness and the most honesty.
**Weak:** "I never avoid things." (Unlikely to be true.) Or defensive — the child does not want to admit avoidance.

### Prompt 4: The Most Courageous Person
**Strong:** Names a specific person — from a story, from their life, or from history. Can explain what made the person courageous. Identifies that the person was afraid and acted anyway.
**Weak:** "My dad because he is strong." Strength is not courage. If the child cannot distinguish physical strength from moral courage, the conceptual foundation is not yet in place.

### Prompt 5: The Hard Thing You Do First
**Strong:** Names a specific daily task. Explains why it is hard. Can describe the experience of getting it done first — does the rest of the day feel different?
**Weak:** "I don't know." The practice is not established if the child cannot identify their hard-thing-first habit.

### Prompt 6: What You Are Still Afraid Of
**Strong:** Honest naming of a real fear. Can assess whether it is getting smaller or bigger. May have an idea for what to do about it. This is the most valuable prompt because it reveals the child's self-awareness about their own fear landscape.
**Weak:** "Nothing." (Almost certainly false at this age.) Or excessive list with no sense of which fears are shrinking — the child is overwhelmed rather than progressing.
